sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Newborn life support focuses on warmth, airway positioning, effective ventilation, and coordinated escalation when a baby fails to breathe or maintain an adequate heart rate after birth. Ventilation is the most important early intervention.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Newborn who is apnoeic, gasping, floppy, poorly perfused, or cyanosed after birth
- Heart rate under 100 beats per minute after initial drying, stimulation, and positioning
- Poor response to drying, stimulation, and airway positioning
- Prematurity, meconium, or complicated birth increasing resuscitation risk

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Warmth and airway positioning are crucial, but effective positive-pressure ventilation is the key intervention when the baby is not breathing adequately
- Routine suction is not recommended unless material is clearly obstructing the airway
- Reassessment should focus on breathing, chest rise, heart rate, colour, and tone
- Neonatal resuscitation usually occurs alongside ongoing maternal care, so both patients need active oversight

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Dry, warm, and stimulate the newborn while maintaining neutral airway position
- Assess heart rate by ECG or auscultation where possible; if unavailable, use the umbilical cord pulse or pulse oximetry as alternatives
- If the baby is not breathing effectively, begin positive-pressure ventilation at 40 to 60 breaths per minute with attention to visible chest rise
- Use room air first for term newborns during the initial phase unless local protocol or deterioration indicates oxygen escalation
- Reassess heart rate and breathing frequently, and if heart rate remains below 60 beats per minute despite effective ventilation, begin neonatal chest compressions using the two-thumb encircling technique at a 3:1 compression-to-ventilation ratio (90 compressions and 30 ventilations per minute) and expedite transport
- If meconium is present and the newborn is not vigorous (absent or poor tone, absent or inadequate breathing, heart rate below 100), prioritise airway and ventilation - do not delay positive pressure ventilation to attempt suction; escalate for advanced airway management early

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Use a two-person ventilation technique and jaw support where scene resourcing permits and mask ventilation is ineffective or inconsistent
- Apply pre-ductal pulse oximetry to the right hand where feasible without interrupting effective ventilation
- Gain IO or IV access only when advanced resuscitation is required and this does not compromise core airway and ventilation priorities
- Administer protocol-led neonatal medications only when indicated after effective ventilation and compressions have been established
- Pre-notify the receiving emergency, neonatal, or obstetric service early and continue active resuscitation during transport where required

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Premature babies are especially vulnerable to heat loss and may need more deliberate thermal protection
- Persistent poor heart rate usually reflects inadequate ventilation until proven otherwise
- Newborn resuscitation principles can be applied through the first 24 hours after birth while transition physiology is still evolving
